title: ESI Overview and Purpose
content: The Emergency Severity Index (ESI) is a five-level triage algorithm used in emergency departments to rapidly categorise patients by acuity and anticipated resource needs. It was developed to standardise triage decisions and improve patient flow, prioritising the sickest patients for immediate care.

title: ESI Level 1 — Immediate Life Threat
content: ESI-1 is assigned to patients requiring immediate life-saving intervention, such as those in cardiac arrest, severe respiratory distress with impending failure, or profound shock. These patients bypass the waiting room entirely and are taken straight to a resuscitation bay.

title: ESI Level 2 — High-Risk Situation
content: ESI-2 patients are in a high-risk situation, are confused, lethargic, or disoriented, or are in severe pain or distress. Examples include suspected stroke, chest pain with cardiac features, and active suicidal ideation with a plan.

title: ESI Level 3 — Stable, Multiple Resources Needed
content: ESI-3 patients are physiologically stable but are expected to require two or more resources, such as laboratory testing and imaging, to reach a diagnosis. These patients can safely wait briefly but require a full diagnostic workup.

title: ESI Level 4 — One Resource Needed
content: ESI-4 patients are stable and require exactly one resource, such as a single X-ray, simple laceration repair, or one type of laboratory test. Vital signs are typically normal and the complaint is non-urgent.

title: ESI Level 5 — No Resources Needed
content: ESI-5 patients require no diagnostic or therapeutic resources beyond a history and physical examination. Typical presentations include minor cold symptoms, a simple rash, or a request for a prescription refill.

title: ESI Decision Point A — Need for Immediate Intervention
content: The first decision point in the ESI algorithm asks whether the patient requires an immediate life-saving intervention right now. A yes answer assigns ESI-1 without proceeding further through the algorithm.

title: ESI Decision Point B — High-Risk Situation
content: If a patient does not meet ESI-1 criteria, the triage nurse asks whether this is a high-risk situation, whether the patient is confused or in severe distress, or whether the patient should not wait. A yes answer assigns ESI-2.

title: ESI Decision Point C — Resource Prediction
content: For patients who do not meet ESI-1 or ESI-2 criteria, the nurse predicts how many resources the patient is likely to need. Zero resources suggests ESI-5, one resource suggests ESI-4, and two or more suggests ESI-3.

title: ESI Decision Point D — Vital Sign Danger Zone
content: For patients predicted to need two or more resources, the nurse checks vital signs against age-based danger zone thresholds. Vital signs outside the danger zone may prompt up-triage from ESI-3 to ESI-2 even without classic high-risk features.

title: ESI Definition of a Resource
content: In the ESI algorithm, a resource is any diagnostic test, procedure, or consultation beyond the history and physical examination that requires staff time and equipment. Counting anticipated resources is central to differentiating ESI-3, ESI-4, and ESI-5.

title: ESI Resources That Count — Laboratory and Imaging
content: Resources counted in ESI resource prediction include laboratory tests, ECG, X-ray, CT, MRI, ultrasound, and angiography. Each distinct imaging or lab category typically counts as one resource toward the ESI-3 threshold of two or more.

title: ESI Resources That Count — IV Fluids and Medications
content: IV, IM, or nebulised medications and IV fluids for hydration each count as a resource in ESI prediction. A patient needing IV fluids plus bloodwork would meet the two-resource threshold for ESI-3.

title: ESI Resources That Count — Procedures and Consultation
content: Specialty consultation, simple procedures such as a laceration repair requiring sedation, and complex procedures such as conscious sedation are counted as resources in the ESI algorithm. Each represents staff time and resource utilisation beyond basic assessment.

title: ESI Resources That Do Not Count
content: History and physical exam, point-of-care testing such as urine dip or glucose, saline or heparin lock placement, oral medications, tetanus immunisation, and prescription refills do not count as resources in the ESI algorithm. These reflect baseline care rather than additional workup.

title: ESI Resources That Do Not Count — Simple Care
content: Simple wound care such as dressing changes, crutches, splints, and slings are not counted as resources in ESI prediction. A patient needing only a splint and crutches typically falls into ESI-4 or ESI-5 depending on additional needs.

title: ESI-1 Clinical Examples
content: Classic ESI-1 presentations include cardiac or respiratory arrest, severe respiratory distress with agonal breathing, unresponsiveness to verbal or painful stimuli, and overdose with significant respiratory depression. All require resuscitative intervention immediately on arrival.

title: ESI-1 in Major Trauma
content: Trauma patients with unstable vital signs, severe airway compromise, or uncontrolled haemorrhage are assigned ESI-1. These patients require simultaneous airway management, haemorrhage control, and rapid transfer to a resuscitation bay or operating theatre.

title: ESI-2 Examples — Suspected STEMI
content: A patient with chest pain, diaphoresis, and ECG changes consistent with ST-elevation myocardial infarction is assigned ESI-2 due to the high-risk situation, even if vital signs remain stable on arrival. Rapid catheterisation laboratory activation is time-critical.

title: ESI-2 Examples — Suspected Stroke
content: A patient presenting with sudden facial droop, arm weakness, and slurred speech within the thrombolysis treatment window is assigned ESI-2. Time-sensitive neurological emergencies require expedited assessment to preserve eligibility for reperfusion therapy.

title: ESI-2 Examples — Suspected Sepsis
content: A patient with fever, tachycardia, and new confusion in the context of a suspected infection is assigned ESI-2 due to the high-risk nature of evolving sepsis. Early recognition at triage supports timely initiation of sepsis bundle care.

title: ESI-2 Examples — Severe Pain
content: Patients reporting severe pain rated 7 or above out of 10, such as renal colic or sickle cell crisis, are commonly assigned ESI-2 because of the distress and clinical urgency, independent of vital sign abnormality.

title: ESI-2 Examples — Active Suicidal Ideation
content: A patient expressing active suicidal ideation with a specific plan and means is assigned ESI-2 due to immediate risk to safety. These patients require continuous observation and rapid psychiatric assessment.

title: ESI-2 Examples — Immunocompromised Fever
content: A patient undergoing chemotherapy who presents with fever is assigned ESI-2 due to the risk of neutropenic sepsis, regardless of how well the patient appears clinically. Rapid antibiotic administration is critical in this population.

title: ESI Vital Sign Danger Zone — Adults
content: For adults, ESI danger zone vital signs include heart rate above 100 bpm, respiratory rate above 20 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation below 92%, and systolic blood pressure below 90 mmHg. Any of these findings should prompt consideration of up-triage to ESI-2.

title: ESI Vital Sign Danger Zone — Temperature
content: Fever above 38.5°C or hypothermia below 35°C is included in the ESI danger zone thresholds. Temperature abnormalities combined with other risk factors such as immunosuppression strengthen the case for up-triage.

title: ESI Pediatric Danger Zone Vital Signs
content: ESI uses age-adjusted vital sign tables for infants, toddlers, and children, since normal heart and respiratory rates differ substantially by age. A heart rate that is normal for an adult may represent significant tachycardia in an infant.

title: ESI — Febrile Infants Under 3 Months
content: Infants under 3 months of age presenting with fever are automatically assigned ESI-2 due to the elevated risk of serious bacterial infection, including meningitis and bacteraemia, in this age group regardless of appearance.

title: ESI — Febrile Toddlers 3 to 36 Months
content: Children aged 3 to 36 months with fever require careful assessment for toxic appearance, lethargy, or poor perfusion. Toxic-appearing febrile toddlers are assigned ESI-2, while well-appearing febrile toddlers may be triaged ESI-3.

title: ESI in Pregnancy — Third Trimester Bleeding
content: Vaginal bleeding in the third trimester of pregnancy is considered a high-risk obstetric emergency and is assigned ESI-2, as it may indicate placental abruption or placenta praevia requiring urgent evaluation.

title: ESI in Pregnancy — Decreased Fetal Movement
content: A pregnant patient reporting decreased or absent fetal movement is triaged as ESI-2 due to concern for fetal compromise, prompting urgent fetal heart rate monitoring and obstetric assessment.

title: ESI — Geriatric Atypical Presentations
content: Elderly patients may present atypically with serious illness, such as silent myocardial infarction or sepsis without fever. Triage nurses are encouraged to maintain a lower threshold for up-triage in elderly patients with vague complaints.

title: ESI — Sickle Cell Vaso-Occlusive Crisis
content: Patients with sickle cell disease presenting in vaso-occlusive pain crisis are typically assigned ESI-2 due to the severity of pain and risk of complications such as acute chest syndrome. Rapid analgesia administration is a quality priority.

title: ESI — Dialysis Patients Presenting Unwell
content: Dialysis-dependent patients who appear unwell, particularly with hyperkalaemia risk or missed dialysis sessions, warrant a lower threshold for ESI-2 assignment due to the potential for rapid cardiac decompensation.

title: ESI and Pain Scores — Not a Sole Determinant
content: While severe pain of 7 or above may suggest ESI-2, pain score alone does not automatically determine acuity level. Clinical judgement combining the source of pain, vital signs, and overall presentation is required.

title: ESI — The 'Across the Room' Assessment
content: Experienced triage nurses often form an initial gestalt impression of acuity within seconds of seeing a patient, sometimes called the 'across the room' assessment. This first impression is then validated through the structured ESI algorithm.

title: ESI Reliability and Validity
content: Multiple studies have demonstrated good inter-rater reliability and predictive validity of the ESI algorithm for hospital admission, resource use, and mortality. Training and ongoing competency assessment for triage staff are essential to maintain this reliability.

title: ESI Implementation — Staff Training
content: Effective ESI implementation requires structured training for triage nurses, including case-based practice and periodic competency review. Inconsistent application of the algorithm can lead to both under-triage and over-triage.

title: ESI Under-Triage Risk
content: Under-triage occurs when a patient is assigned a lower acuity level than their true clinical risk warrants, potentially delaying necessary care. Vague presentations of sepsis, ectopic pregnancy, and aortic dissection are recognised under-triage risks.

title: ESI Over-Triage and Resource Strain
content: Over-triage assigns a higher acuity level than clinically necessary, consuming limited monitored bed resources and potentially delaying care for genuinely high-acuity patients. Balanced triage accuracy supports efficient department flow.

title: ESI Level Distribution in a Typical Emergency Department
content: In most emergency departments, ESI-3 is the most commonly assigned level, followed by ESI-2 and ESI-4, with ESI-1 and ESI-5 representing the smallest proportions of presentations. This distribution informs staffing and resource planning.

title: ESI Level and Hospital Admission Correlation
content: ESI level correlates with likelihood of hospital admission: ESI-1 and ESI-2 patients are admitted at substantially higher rates than ESI-4 and ESI-5 patients, supporting the algorithm's predictive validity for resource use.

title: ESI and Left Without Being Seen Rates
content: Patients triaged as lower acuity, particularly ESI-4 and ESI-5, are more likely to leave without being seen during periods of department crowding. Monitoring this metric by triage level helps identify safety and flow concerns.

title: ESI Is Not a Disaster Triage Tool
content: The ESI algorithm is designed for individual patient triage in routine emergency department operations and is not intended for mass casualty or disaster triage, where tools such as START (Simple Triage and Rapid Treatment) are used instead.

title: ESI Re-Triage After Condition Change
content: Patients whose clinical condition changes while waiting must be re-triaged promptly. A patient initially assigned ESI-3 who develops new chest pain or altered mental status should be reassessed and may be upgraded to ESI-2 or ESI-1.

title: ESI Reassessment Intervals by Level
content: Patients assigned ESI-2 typically require reassessment every 10 to 15 minutes while waiting, ESI-3 patients every 30 minutes, and ESI-4 and ESI-5 patients at lower frequency, reflecting differing risks of deterioration.

title: ESI Documentation Requirements
content: Triage documentation under the ESI framework should record the assigned level, the rationale including decision points used, vital signs, presenting complaint, and time of assessment, supporting both clinical handover and quality review.

title: ESI Compared with Three- and Four-Level Triage Systems
content: The five-level ESI algorithm offers greater discrimination of acuity than older three-level (emergent, urgent, non-urgent) or four-level systems, improving prioritisation accuracy and resource planning in busy emergency departments.

title: ESI in Toxicological Presentations
content: Patients with suspected overdose or poisoning are risk-stratified based on the substance involved, time since ingestion, and current vital signs. Significant respiratory depression or haemodynamic instability warrants ESI-1, while asymptomatic recent ingestion may be ESI-2 pending observation.

title: ESI in Burn Patients
content: Burn severity, percentage of body surface area involved, and airway involvement determine ESI level in burn patients. Significant airway burns or large surface area burns with haemodynamic compromise warrant ESI-1 or ESI-2.

title: ESI Resource Counting — Abdominal Pain Example
content: A patient with abdominal pain who is expected to need bloodwork and a CT scan meets the two-resource threshold and is typically assigned ESI-3, provided vital signs remain within the safe range.

title: ESI Resource Counting — Laceration Example
content: A patient with a simple laceration requiring only suture repair, and no imaging or labs, is typically assigned ESI-4, as a single procedural resource is anticipated.

title: ESI Resource Counting — Minor Complaint Example
content: A patient requesting a medication refill or seeking advice for a minor cold with no abnormal findings is typically assigned ESI-5, since no diagnostic or therapeutic resources beyond history and examination are anticipated.

title: ESI and Department Disposition Planning
content: ESI level helps emergency departments plan bed assignment and staffing in real time. ESI-1 and ESI-2 patients are directed to resuscitation or monitored bays, while ESI-4 and ESI-5 patients may be managed in fast-track or ambulatory areas.

title: ESI Algorithm Version History
content: The ESI algorithm has been refined through several versions, most notably version 4, which clarified resource definitions and added detailed guidance for special populations such as paediatric and psychiatric patients to improve consistency.

title: ESI and Return Visits Within 72 Hours
content: Patients returning to the emergency department within 72 hours of a prior visit for the same or a related complaint should be carefully reassessed, as this pattern can indicate missed or evolving serious pathology and may warrant up-triage.

title: ESI — Patients Who 'Cannot Wait Safely'
content: A core question within ESI Decision Point B is whether the patient can safely wait to be seen. Patients who clearly cannot wait without risk of deterioration are assigned ESI-2 even without a single classic high-risk diagnosis.

title: ESI and New Onset Confusion
content: New onset confusion, lethargy, or disorientation at triage is treated as a high-risk indicator under ESI Decision Point B, as it may reflect sepsis, stroke, hypoglycaemia, or other serious underlying pathology.

title: ESI-1 — Intubated or Ventilated Patients
content: Patients arriving already intubated, mechanically ventilated, or requiring bag-mask ventilation are automatically assigned ESI-1, reflecting the immediate and ongoing need for airway and ventilatory support.

title: ESI-1 — Severe Bradycardia or Tachycardia with Hypoperfusion
content: Patients with severe bradycardia or tachycardia accompanied by signs of poor perfusion, such as altered mental status or hypotension, are assigned ESI-1 due to the immediate risk of cardiovascular collapse.

title: ESI Role of the Triage Nurse
content: The triage nurse applies the ESI algorithm using a brief focused assessment, vital signs, and clinical judgement to assign an acuity level within minutes of patient arrival, without performing a full diagnostic workup at this stage.

title: ESI and Crowding-Related Up-Triage
content: During periods of severe department crowding, some systems apply more conservative thresholds for up-triage to ensure at-risk patients are identified despite longer waiting times, although this is not part of the original ESI algorithm itself.

title: ESI — Multiple High-Risk Features Compounding Acuity
content: When a patient presents with several concerning features simultaneously, such as abnormal vital signs plus severe pain plus a high-risk history, the cumulative concern should prompt assignment of the higher acuity level rather than averaging the individual concerns.

title: ESI — Gastrointestinal Bleeding Risk Stratification
content: A patient with gastrointestinal bleeding who is haemodynamically stable with normal vital signs may be assigned ESI-3 pending workup, whereas one with tachycardia, hypotension, or large volume bleeding is assigned ESI-1 or ESI-2.

title: ESI and Abdominal Pain in Elderly Patients
content: Abdominal pain in elderly patients carries a higher risk of serious pathology such as mesenteric ischaemia or perforation, and triage nurses are encouraged to apply a lower threshold for up-triage in this population compared with younger adults.

title: ESI Algorithm Flow Summary
content: The ESI algorithm proceeds through four sequential decision points: need for immediate life-saving intervention, high-risk situation or severe distress, predicted resource needs, and vital sign danger zone, assigning the patient to a level as soon as a criterion is met.

title: ESI and Anticoagulant Use in Trauma
content: Patients on anticoagulant or antiplatelet medications presenting after even minor head trauma are considered higher risk for intracranial haemorrhage and are often up-triaged to ESI-2 to expedite imaging.

title: ESI and Allergic Reaction Severity
content: A mild localised allergic reaction without airway or systemic involvement may be assigned ESI-4, whereas signs of anaphylaxis such as wheeze, stridor, or hypotension require assignment of ESI-1 or ESI-2.

title: ESI and Anaphylaxis
content: Anaphylaxis with airway compromise, significant bronchospasm, or haemodynamic instability is assigned ESI-1, reflecting the need for immediate intramuscular adrenaline and resuscitative care on arrival.

title: ESI and Headache Risk Stratification
content: Most headaches are low acuity, but sudden-onset severe 'thunderclap' headache, headache with neurological deficit, or headache with fever and neck stiffness are red flags warranting ESI-2 assignment due to concern for subarachnoid haemorrhage or meningitis.

title: ESI and Simple Musculoskeletal Injuries
content: Isolated minor musculoskeletal injuries without deformity, neurovascular compromise, or significant pain, such as a minor ankle sprain, are typically assigned ESI-4 or ESI-5 depending on whether imaging is anticipated.

title: ESI and Open Fracture or Neurovascular Compromise
content: An open fracture, a fracture with absent distal pulses, or significant deformity with neurovascular compromise warrants ESI-2 due to the time-sensitive risk of limb ischaemia or infection.

title: ESI and Eye Emergencies
content: Chemical eye exposure and sudden painless vision loss are treated as high-risk presentations requiring immediate irrigation or rapid ophthalmology assessment respectively, and are typically assigned ESI-2.

title: ESI and Testicular Torsion
content: Sudden severe testicular pain, particularly in adolescent males, is treated as a time-critical surgical emergency due to the risk of testicular loss with delayed detorsion, and is assigned ESI-2.

title: ESI and Dental or Minor ENT Complaints
content: Uncomplicated dental pain or minor ENT complaints such as earache without systemic features typically require minimal resources and are assigned ESI-4 or ESI-5.

title: ESI and Suspected Domestic Violence or Abuse
content: Patients presenting with injuries suggestive of abuse require careful, sensitive assessment; while the physical acuity may be low, safety concerns can warrant a higher overall priority for assessment and safeguarding referral.

title: ESI and the Limits of Vital Signs Alone
content: Normal vital signs do not exclude a high-risk situation; conditions such as ectopic pregnancy or early sepsis can present with normal vital signs initially, so ESI relies on clinical gestalt in addition to numeric thresholds.

title: ESI Quality Improvement Use
content: ESI level distribution and time-to-triage metrics are commonly used in emergency department quality improvement programmes to monitor patient safety, identify bottlenecks, and benchmark performance against peer institutions.

title: NEWS2 — Overview and Purpose
content: The National Early Warning Score 2 (NEWS2) is a standardised physiological scoring system used to detect clinical deterioration in acutely ill adult patients. It is recommended by the Royal College of Physicians and widely adopted across UK hospitals and ambulance services as a common early warning language.

title: NEWS2 — Six Physiological Parameters
content: NEWS2 is calculated from six parameters: respiration rate, oxygen saturation, systolic blood pressure, pulse rate, level of consciousness, and temperature. Each parameter is scored from 0 to 3 based on deviation from a defined normal range, and the scores are summed.

title: NEWS2 — Respiration Rate Scoring
content: A respiration rate of 12 to 20 breaths per minute scores 0. Rates of 9 to 11 score 1, rates of 21 to 24 score 2, and rates of 8 or below or 25 or above score 3, reflecting tachypnoea or bradypnoea as early markers of deterioration.

title: NEWS2 — Oxygen Saturation Scale 1
content: Scale 1 is used for most patients without hypercapnic respiratory failure. SpO2 of 96% or above scores 0, 94 to 95% scores 1, 92 to 93% scores 2, and 91% or below scores 3.

title: NEWS2 — Oxygen Saturation Scale 2
content: Scale 2 is used for patients with confirmed hypercapnic respiratory failure, such as severe COPD, with a target saturation range of 88 to 92%. Selecting the wrong scale can cause inappropriate over- or under-scoring of clinical risk.

title: NEWS2 — Systolic Blood Pressure Scoring
content: A systolic blood pressure of 111 to 219 mmHg scores 0. Values of 101 to 110 score 1, 91 to 100 score 2, and 90 or below or 220 or above score 3, capturing both hypotension and severe hypertension as risk markers.

title: NEWS2 — Pulse Rate Scoring
content: A pulse rate of 51 to 90 beats per minute scores 0. Rates of 41 to 50 or 91 to 110 score 1, 111 to 130 score 2, and 40 or below or 131 or above score 3, reflecting both bradycardia and tachycardia as concerning findings.

title: NEWS2 — ACVPU Consciousness Scale
content: NEWS2 assesses consciousness using ACVPU: Alert scores 0, while any new Confusion, Voice-responsive, Pain-responsive, or Unresponsive state scores 3. New confusion is a particularly important red flag suggesting sepsis or acute neurological change.

title: NEWS2 — Temperature Scoring
content: A temperature of 36.1 to 38.0°C scores 0. Temperatures of 35.1 to 36.0°C or 38.1 to 39.0°C score 1, 39.1°C or above scores 2, and 35.0°C or below scores 3, since both hypothermia and high fever indicate physiological stress.

title: NEWS2 — Supplemental Oxygen Score
content: A patient requiring any supplemental oxygen to maintain target saturation automatically receives an additional 2 points in NEWS2, regardless of the actual SpO2 reading achieved, reflecting the underlying physiological burden of needing oxygen support.

title: NEWS2 — Low Risk Score Band (0 to 4)
content: A total NEWS2 score of 0 to 4, with no single parameter scoring 3, indicates low clinical risk. These patients require routine ward-based monitoring, typically every 12 hours, unless clinical concern dictates otherwise.

title: NEWS2 — Medium Risk Score Band (5 to 6 or Red Score)
content: A total NEWS2 score of 5 to 6, or any single parameter scoring 3 (known as a 'red score'), indicates medium clinical risk. These patients require urgent review by a clinician competent in assessing acute illness, typically within 30 to 60 minutes.

title: NEWS2 — High Risk Score Band (7 or Above)
content: A total NEWS2 score of 7 or above indicates high clinical risk and requires an emergency response within minutes, including assessment by a clinician with critical care competencies and consideration of escalation to a higher level of care.

title: NEWS2 — Single Red Score Significance
content: A single parameter scoring 3 points, called a red score, triggers a medium-risk response even if the total NEWS2 score is otherwise low, because an extreme deviation in even one vital sign can indicate serious underlying illness.

title: NEWS2 — Monitoring Frequency by Score
content: Monitoring frequency increases with NEWS2 score: a score of 0 allows 12-hourly observations, 1 to 4 requires 4 to 6 hourly checks, 5 to 6 requires hourly or continuous monitoring, and 7 or above requires continuous monitoring, ideally in a higher level of care setting.

title: NEWS2 and Sepsis Recognition
content: NEWS2 is widely used alongside sepsis screening tools because a deteriorating NEWS2 score in a patient with suspected infection should prompt urgent consideration of sepsis and initiation of the sepsis care bundle.

title: NEWS2 in COPD Patients
content: NEWS2 includes a dedicated Scale 2 for oxygen saturation in patients with confirmed hypercapnic respiratory failure, most commonly due to COPD, to avoid inappropriately high scores from a deliberately lower target saturation range of 88 to 92%.

title: NEWS2 Limitations in Pregnancy
content: NEWS2 is not validated for use in pregnant patients, as normal physiological changes in pregnancy, such as increased resting heart rate, can produce misleading scores. A modified obstetric early warning score should be used instead.

title: NEWS2 Not Validated for Children
content: NEWS2 is designed for use in adults aged 16 years and over. Paediatric early warning scores, which use age-specific vital sign ranges, should be used for children, as adult thresholds are not appropriate for younger patients.

title: NEWS2 Escalation Protocol Structure
content: Most NEWS2-based escalation protocols define specific actions at each risk band, such as which staff member must review the patient, the maximum time to review, and whether outreach or critical care teams should be notified.

title: NEWS2 and Critical Care Outreach Teams
content: A NEWS2 score of 7 or above, or persistent deterioration despite initial treatment, commonly triggers referral to a critical care outreach team for assessment of escalation needs, including potential transfer to a higher level of care.

title: NEWS2 — Trend Monitoring Over Time
content: A rising NEWS2 score over successive observations, even if each individual score remains in the medium-risk band, is clinically significant and should prompt clinical review rather than being dismissed as stable.

title: NEWS2 Documentation Standards
content: Each NEWS2 observation set should be documented with the time recorded, individual parameter scores, the aggregate score, the escalation action taken, and the name of the assessing clinician to support clinical governance and audit.

title: NEWS2 Communication — SBAR Handover
content: When escalating a deteriorating patient based on NEWS2, the SBAR framework (Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation) is recommended to ensure structured, clear communication between nursing and medical staff.

title: NEWS2 Use in Ambulance and Pre-Hospital Settings
content: NEWS2 is used by ambulance services to standardise communication of patient acuity from the pre-hospital setting to the receiving emergency department, supporting consistent handover and appropriate destination decisions.

title: NEWS2 Use in Primary Care Referral
content: General practitioners and community clinicians increasingly use NEWS2 to support decisions about whether to refer an acutely unwell patient to hospital, providing a shared language of acuity between care settings.

title: NEWS2 Ceiling of Care Considerations
content: In patients with an agreed ceiling of care or palliative care plan, escalation actions triggered by NEWS2 should be considered alongside the patient's wishes and treatment plan rather than applied automatically, since not all escalation is appropriate or wanted.

title: NEWS2 Compared with NEWS1
content: NEWS2 improved on the original NEWS by adding the SpO2 Scale 2 for hypercapnic respiratory failure and by giving new confusion equal weight to other forms of altered consciousness, increasing sensitivity for sepsis-related deterioration.

title: NEWS2 Validation Evidence
content: NEWS2 has been validated in large cohort studies as a predictor of in-hospital mortality, ICU admission, and cardiac arrest, with higher scores associated with progressively greater risk of adverse outcomes within 24 hours.

title: NEWS2 and Staff Training Requirements
content: Accurate use of NEWS2 requires staff training in correct measurement technique for each vital sign, selection of the correct SpO2 scale, and consistent application of the ACVPU consciousness assessment to avoid scoring errors.

title: NEWS2 Chart Design and Aggregate Scoring
content: NEWS2 observation charts use a colour-coded format, typically red, yellow, and white zones, to visually flag abnormal parameters at the bedside, making it easier for staff to recognise an evolving high score at a glance.

title: NEWS2 and Acute Respiratory Failure
content: A markedly elevated NEWS2 score driven primarily by respiratory rate and oxygen saturation abnormalities should prompt urgent assessment for acute respiratory failure, including arterial blood gas analysis and consideration of ventilatory support.

title: NEWS2 and Cardiac Arrhythmia Detection
content: Marked pulse rate abnormalities captured by NEWS2, particularly severe tachycardia or bradycardia, should prompt ECG assessment to identify arrhythmias that may require urgent treatment.

title: NEWS2 — Minimum Dataset for Scoring
content: A complete NEWS2 score requires all six parameters to be measured; an incomplete set of observations, such as a missing respiratory rate, can falsely lower the apparent score and mask deterioration.

title: NEWS2 and Clinical Judgement Override
content: Clinical judgement should always be able to override a low NEWS2 score if a clinician has specific concern about a patient, since the score is a screening tool and does not capture every relevant clinical factor.

title: NEWS2 Maximum Possible Score
content: The maximum possible NEWS2 score is 20, combining the highest possible scores across all six parameters plus the supplemental oxygen score, although such an extreme combination would represent profound physiological compromise.

title: NEWS2 — Frequency of Recalculation After Intervention
content: NEWS2 should be recalculated after any clinical intervention, such as fluid administration or oxygen therapy adjustment, to assess the patient's response to treatment and guide further escalation decisions.

title: NEWS2 Role in Deteriorating Patient Pathways
content: NEWS2 forms the foundation of many hospital deteriorating patient pathways, triggering a structured sequence of nurse assessment, doctor review, and senior or critical care escalation as the score rises through defined thresholds.

title: NEWS2 Post-Operative Monitoring
content: NEWS2 is commonly used for post-operative ward monitoring to detect early signs of complications such as haemorrhage, sepsis, or respiratory compromise before they become clinically obvious through other means.

title: NEWS2 and Blood Glucose Considerations
content: Although blood glucose is not part of the core NEWS2 calculation, hypoglycaemia should always be excluded in any patient with new confusion or altered consciousness scoring on the ACVPU scale, as it is a rapidly reversible cause.

title: NEWS2 in Patients with Chronic Baseline Abnormalities
content: Patients with chronic conditions such as atrial fibrillation with a persistently elevated heart rate, or chronic hypoxaemia, may have an elevated baseline NEWS2 score; clinicians should consider the patient's usual baseline when interpreting an elevated score.

title: NEWS2 and Time to Escalation Auditing
content: Hospitals commonly audit the time between a high NEWS2 score being recorded and the corresponding clinical review taking place, using this metric to identify and address delays in the deteriorating patient pathway.

title: NEWS2 and Family or Carer Concern
content: Some deteriorating patient protocols incorporate family or carer concern as an additional trigger for escalation alongside the numerical NEWS2 score, recognising that those close to the patient may notice subtle changes before they appear in vital signs.

title: NEWS2 and Electronic Early Warning Systems
content: Many hospitals have implemented electronic systems that automatically calculate NEWS2 from entered vital signs and generate alerts to nursing and medical staff when escalation thresholds are reached, reducing calculation errors.

title: NEWS2 Scale Selection Errors
content: A common error in NEWS2 application is failing to switch to Scale 2 for patients with confirmed hypercapnic respiratory failure, which can result in an inappropriately high score and unnecessary escalation in a clinically stable COPD patient.

title: NEWS2 — Distinguishing Baseline Confusion from New Confusion
content: NEWS2 specifically scores new confusion, not chronic baseline confusion such as established dementia. Clinicians must establish the patient's baseline mental state to correctly interpret the ACVPU component of the score.

title: NEWS2 Use Across Care Transitions
content: Recording NEWS2 at points of care transition, such as transfer between wards or hospitals, helps ensure consistent recognition of deterioration risk and supports safe handover of clinical responsibility.

title: NEWS2 and Sepsis Six Bundle Integration
content: Many sepsis pathways use a NEWS2 score of 5 or above in combination with suspected infection as a trigger to commence the Sepsis Six bundle, integrating early warning scoring directly with time-critical treatment protocols.

title: NEWS2 — Importance of Accurate Respiratory Rate Counting
content: Respiratory rate should be counted manually over a full 60 seconds rather than estimated, as it is one of the earliest and most sensitive indicators of physiological deterioration within the NEWS2 score.

title: NEWS2 in End-of-Life Care Planning
content: For patients approaching the end of life, a rising NEWS2 score may be expected as part of the natural disease trajectory, and care teams should align escalation responses with the patient's documented goals of care.

title: Sepsis-3 Definition
content: Sepsis is defined by the Sepsis-3 international consensus as life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection. This definition replaced the older SIRS-based definition to better reflect the underlying pathophysiology and associated mortality risk.

title: Septic Shock Definition
content: Septic shock is a subset of sepsis with profound circulatory, cellular, and metabolic abnormalities. It is identified clinically by the need for vasopressors to maintain mean arterial pressure of 65 mmHg or above, together with a serum lactate above 2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation.

title: SOFA Score and Organ Dysfunction
content: The Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (SOFA) score evaluates six organ systems: respiratory, cardiovascular, hepatic, coagulation, renal, and neurological. An acute increase of 2 points or more in a patient with infection defines sepsis under the Sepsis-3 criteria.

title: qSOFA Score for Bedside Screening
content: The quick SOFA (qSOFA) score is a rapid bedside tool assigning one point each for altered mentation, respiratory rate of 22 or above, and systolic blood pressure of 100 mmHg or below. A score of 2 or more identifies patients at higher risk of poor outcome and warrants further assessment.

title: SIRS Criteria
content: Systemic Inflammatory Response Syndrome (SIRS) criteria include temperature above 38°C or below 36°C, heart rate above 90 bpm, respiratory rate above 20 per minute, and abnormal white cell count. Two or more criteria with suspected infection raise concern for sepsis, though SIRS alone has limited specificity.

title: Serum Lactate as a Sepsis Marker
content: Serum lactate should be measured in all patients with suspected sepsis. A lactate above 2 mmol/L indicates tissue hypoperfusion and is associated with increased mortality, even without overt hypotension, while a lactate above 4 mmol/L signals critical illness.

title: Sepsis Six Care Bundle
content: The Sepsis Six bundle should be completed within one hour of sepsis recognition: give high-flow oxygen, take blood cultures, give intravenous antibiotics, give an intravenous fluid bolus, check lactate and haemoglobin, and monitor urine output. Timely completion is associated with reduced mortality.

title: Antibiotic Timing in Sepsis
content: Broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics should be given within one hour of sepsis recognition, and immediately in septic shock. Each hour of delay is associated with increased mortality, so antibiotics should not be delayed to obtain blood cultures.

title: Blood Cultures Before Antibiotics
content: Blood cultures should ideally be drawn before antibiotic administration to maximise diagnostic yield, but the priority is timely antibiotic delivery; antibiotics must never be delayed solely to obtain cultures in suspected septic shock.

title: Fluid Resuscitation in Sepsis
content: Current guidance recommends 30 mL/kg of intravenous crystalloid fluid within the first three hours for sepsis-induced hypoperfusion, with reassessment using dynamic measures of fluid responsiveness to avoid over-resuscitation.

title: Fluid Overload Risk in Sepsis Management
content: Excessive fluid administration in sepsis can cause pulmonary oedema and worsen outcomes, particularly in patients with cardiac or renal impairment. Reassessment after each fluid bolus using clinical signs or dynamic measures helps avoid harm from over-resuscitation.

title: Organ Dysfunction Markers in Sepsis
content: Signs of organ dysfunction include acute change in mental status, creatinine rise above 176 micromol/L, bilirubin above 34 micromol/L, platelet count below 100,000, INR above 1.5, and PaO2/FiO2 ratio below 300. Any of these in suspected infection should raise concern for sepsis.

title: Vasopressor Use in Septic Shock
content: Norepinephrine is the first-line vasopressor in septic shock, targeting a mean arterial pressure of 65 mmHg or above. Vasopressin may be added as a second agent, while dopamine is generally avoided due to arrhythmia risk.

title: Source Control in Sepsis Management
content: Identifying and controlling the source of infection, such as draining an abscess, removing an infected device, or surgically managing perforated bowel, is essential alongside antibiotics and fluids in sepsis management, as antibiotics alone may be insufficient.

title: Sepsis Recognition in Children
content: Paediatric sepsis recognition uses age-adjusted vital sign thresholds, since normal heart and respiratory rates differ from adults. Red flag features include non-blanching rash, mottled or ashen appearance, grunting, and reduced consciousness.

title: Sepsis in Infants Under 3 Months
content: Infants under 3 months with fever are at elevated risk of serious bacterial infection, including sepsis and meningitis, and require urgent full septic screen including blood cultures, urine, and consideration of lumbar puncture.

title: Sepsis in Pregnancy and the Postpartum Period
content: Maternal sepsis remains a leading cause of maternal mortality. Pregnancy-adjusted vital sign thresholds should be used, as normal pregnancy physiology can mask early signs of deterioration. Early recognition and the same hour-one bundle principles apply.

title: Neutropenic Sepsis
content: Patients undergoing chemotherapy who present with fever and neutropenia are at high risk of rapid deterioration and require immediate broad-spectrum antibiotics, typically within one hour, regardless of how well they initially appear.

title: Urinary Tract Source of Sepsis
content: Urosepsis is a common cause of sepsis, particularly in elderly patients and those with urinary catheters or obstruction. Urinalysis, urine culture, and consideration of renal imaging for obstruction are key components of the workup.

title: Pneumonia as a Source of Sepsis
content: Community-acquired pneumonia is a leading cause of sepsis. Chest X-ray, blood and sputum cultures, and severity scoring such as CURB-65 help guide initial management alongside the standard sepsis bundle.

title: Intra-Abdominal Source of Sepsis
content: Intra-abdominal infections such as perforated viscus, cholangitis, or appendiceal abscess can rapidly progress to septic shock. Early surgical or interventional radiology consultation for source control is critical alongside antibiotics and fluids.

title: Necrotising Fasciitis and Skin Source Sepsis
content: Necrotising soft tissue infection presents with disproportionate pain, rapidly spreading erythema, skin discolouration, and systemic toxicity. It is a surgical emergency requiring urgent debridement in addition to broad-spectrum antibiotics, as delay significantly increases mortality.

title: Meningococcal Sepsis
content: Meningococcal sepsis presents with fever, a non-blanching purpuric rash, and rapid deterioration. It is a medical emergency requiring immediate antibiotic administration, even before hospital transfer in suspected cases, due to extremely rapid progression.

title: NICE High-Risk Sepsis Criteria
content: High-risk criteria for sepsis include objective evidence of new altered mental state, respiratory rate of 25 or above, need for oxygen to maintain saturation above 92%, systolic blood pressure of 90 mmHg or below, heart rate above 130 bpm, not passed urine in 18 hours, and mottled or ashen skin.

title: NICE Moderate-to-High-Risk (Amber Flag) Sepsis Criteria
content: Moderate-to-high-risk amber flag criteria include relatives' concern about mental status, acute deterioration of functional ability, immunosuppression, trauma or surgery within the past 8 weeks, respiratory rate of 21 to 24, and reduced urine output.

title: Sepsis Red Flag Recognition Tools
content: Validated sepsis screening tools, including NICE criteria, the Sepsis Trust six red flags, and NEWS2 combined with suspected infection, are designed to standardise rapid recognition of sepsis at first clinical contact, before formal SOFA scoring is feasible.

title: Comparison of qSOFA, SIRS, and NEWS2 for Sepsis Screening
content: qSOFA has higher specificity but lower sensitivity than SIRS for predicting sepsis mortality, while NEWS2 combined with suspected infection has been shown in several studies to outperform both for identifying patients at risk of deterioration.

title: The Golden Hour Concept in Sepsis
content: The 'golden hour' in sepsis refers to the first 60 minutes after recognition, during which antibiotic administration, fluid resuscitation, and lactate measurement should be completed to maximise the chance of survival.

title: Sepsis in Elderly Patients
content: Elderly patients with sepsis often present atypically, without fever or marked tachycardia, and may present primarily with confusion, falls, or generalised decline. A lower threshold for sepsis screening is recommended in this population.

title: Altered Mental Status as a Sepsis Indicator
content: New confusion or reduced consciousness in a patient with suspected infection is a key red flag for sepsis and contributes to both the qSOFA score and standard sepsis screening tools, reflecting cerebral hypoperfusion or encephalopathy.

title: Causes of Elevated Lactate Other Than Sepsis
content: Elevated lactate can also result from seizures, severe exercise, liver failure, certain medications such as metformin, and tissue ischaemia unrelated to infection. Clinical context is essential when interpreting an elevated lactate in a patient without obvious infection.

title: Repeat Lactate Measurement in Sepsis
content: Lactate should be remeasured within 2 to 4 hours of initial resuscitation. Failure of lactate to clear, or a rising lactate despite treatment, indicates inadequate resuscitation or ongoing tissue hypoperfusion and should prompt escalation of care.

title: Central Line-Associated Bloodstream Infection
content: Patients with central venous catheters who develop fever and clinical deterioration should be assessed for catheter-related bloodstream infection, with blood cultures drawn from both the line and a peripheral site, and consideration given to line removal.

title: Sepsis Mortality Statistics
content: Sepsis is associated with substantial mortality, with septic shock carrying a hospital mortality exceeding 40% in many series. Early recognition and bundle compliance have been consistently associated with improved survival.

title: Procalcitonin as a Sepsis Biomarker
content: Procalcitonin rises more specifically in bacterial infection than C-reactive protein and can help support a diagnosis of sepsis or guide antibiotic duration, although it should be interpreted alongside clinical assessment rather than in isolation.

title: C-Reactive Protein and White Cell Count in Sepsis
content: C-reactive protein and white cell count are commonly elevated in sepsis but lack specificity, as both can be raised in non-infectious inflammatory conditions. They support but do not confirm a diagnosis of sepsis on their own.

title: ICU Admission Criteria in Sepsis
content: Indications for intensive care admission in sepsis include need for vasopressor support, mechanical ventilation, persistent hypotension despite fluids, or multi-organ dysfunction. Early critical care involvement improves outcomes in septic shock.

title: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation in Sepsis
content: Severe sepsis can trigger disseminated intravascular coagulation, characterised by simultaneous bleeding and clotting due to consumption of clotting factors. Laboratory findings include low platelets, prolonged INR, low fibrinogen, and elevated D-dimer.

title: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome as a Sepsis Complication
content: Sepsis is a common precipitant of acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), characterised by bilateral infiltrates and severe hypoxaemia not explained by cardiac failure. Lung-protective ventilation strategies are used in mechanically ventilated patients with ARDS.

title: Imaging for Source Identification in Sepsis
content: CT imaging is frequently used to identify the source of infection in sepsis when the clinical source is unclear, particularly for suspected intra-abdominal or retroperitoneal infections requiring source control.

title: Antibiotic Selection in Sepsis
content: Empirical antibiotic choice in sepsis should be broad-spectrum, guided by suspected source, local resistance patterns, and patient allergy history, with de-escalation to narrower-spectrum agents once culture results and sensitivities are available.

title: Antibiotic De-Escalation in Sepsis
content: Once culture and sensitivity results are available, antibiotic therapy should be de-escalated to the narrowest effective agent to reduce the risk of antimicrobial resistance and adverse effects, typically reviewed at 48 to 72 hours.

title: Sepsis in Nursing Home and Care Home Residents
content: Care home residents are at higher risk of sepsis due to comorbidity, indwelling devices, and reduced physiological reserve. Atypical presentation, such as falls or reduced oral intake without fever, is common and should prompt sepsis screening.

title: Post-Sepsis Syndrome
content: Survivors of severe sepsis or septic shock may experience post-sepsis syndrome, including physical weakness, cognitive impairment, and psychological symptoms such as anxiety or post-traumatic stress, persisting for months after recovery from the acute illness.

title: Sepsis Bundle Compliance Auditing
content: Hospitals routinely audit compliance with sepsis bundle elements, including time to antibiotics, time to lactate measurement, and time to fluid bolus, using these metrics to drive quality improvement initiatives and reduce preventable mortality.

title: Defining 'Time Zero' in Sepsis Recognition
content: 'Time zero' refers to the moment sepsis is first recognised or suspected, either by triage screening or clinical assessment, and serves as the reference point against which all bundle element timings are measured.

title: Assessing for 'Suspicion of Infection'
content: A structured suspicion-of-infection assessment, considering recent symptoms, examination findings, and risk factors, is a prerequisite step before applying qSOFA or SOFA criteria, since these scores are only valid in the context of confirmed or suspected infection.

title: Sepsis Following Burn Injury
content: Burn patients are at elevated risk of sepsis due to loss of the skin barrier and immune dysregulation. Signs of sepsis in burns can be masked by the hypermetabolic response to injury, requiring careful interpretation of vital signs and laboratory trends.

title: Post-Surgical Sepsis
content: Sepsis following surgery may indicate an anastomotic leak, surgical site infection, or other complication. New fever, tachycardia, or rising lactate in the post-operative period should prompt urgent reassessment and consideration of imaging.

title: Sepsis-3 Criticism and Limitations
content: The Sepsis-3 definition has been criticised for reduced sensitivity in early disease compared with SIRS, as qSOFA may not flag deterioration until organ dysfunction is already established. Many systems therefore use multiple screening tools in parallel.

title: Sepsis Awareness and Public Education
content: Public awareness campaigns for sepsis emphasise recognising symptoms such as slurred speech, extreme shivering, passing no urine, severe breathlessness, and feeling like one might die, encouraging early presentation to emergency care.

title: Sepsis and Hypoglycaemia
content: Hypoglycaemia can occur in severe sepsis, particularly in young children and patients with liver dysfunction, and should be checked and corrected promptly as part of the initial assessment, since it can independently cause altered mental status.

title: Reassessment After the Initial Sepsis Bundle
content: Following completion of the initial hour-one bundle, patients with sepsis require ongoing reassessment of vital signs, urine output, and laboratory trends to determine whether escalation of care, such as ICU referral, is required.

title: Sepsis in Patients with Liver Disease
content: Patients with cirrhosis are at increased risk of sepsis, including spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, and may have a blunted febrile response, making vigilance and a low threshold for screening particularly important in this population.

title: Sepsis in Diabetic Patients
content: Diabetic patients are at higher risk of certain infections, including diabetic foot infections and urinary sepsis, and may have impaired immune response, increasing the risk of rapid progression to severe sepsis if not promptly treated.

title: Chest Pain — Initial Assessment
content: All patients presenting with chest pain should have a 12-lead ECG performed and interpreted within 10 minutes of arrival. Initial assessment should focus on identifying time-critical diagnoses such as STEMI, aortic dissection, and pulmonary embolism.

title: STEMI ECG Criteria
content: ST-elevation myocardial infarction is diagnosed by new ST elevation at the J point in two contiguous leads, with specific millimetre thresholds varying by lead location and patient sex. New left bundle branch block with a compatible clinical picture is treated as a STEMI equivalent.

title: Door-to-Balloon Time
content: For STEMI patients undergoing primary percutaneous coronary intervention, the target door-to-balloon time is 90 minutes from arrival to first device deployment, reflecting the time-critical nature of myocardial reperfusion.

title: Fibrinolysis When PCI Is Not Available
content: When timely primary PCI cannot be achieved within guideline-recommended timeframes, fibrinolytic therapy should be considered for eligible STEMI patients, followed by transfer to a PCI-capable centre for rescue or routine angiography.

title: NSTEMI and Unstable Angina
content: Non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction is diagnosed by elevated cardiac troponin with a clinical presentation consistent with acute coronary syndrome but without ST elevation, while unstable angina presents similarly but with normal troponin. Risk stratification guides timing of invasive management.

title: High-Sensitivity Troponin Testing
content: High-sensitivity troponin assays allow earlier rule-out of myocardial infarction using 0-hour and 1-hour or 0-hour and 3-hour sampling protocols, reducing the time needed to safely discharge low-risk chest pain patients compared with older assays.

title: HEART Score for Chest Pain Risk Stratification
content: The HEART score evaluates History, ECG, Age, Risk factors, and Troponin to stratify patients with chest pain into low, moderate, and high risk categories for major adverse cardiac events, supporting safe early discharge of low-risk patients.

title: TIMI Risk Score
content: The TIMI risk score for unstable angina and NSTEMI incorporates age, risk factors, prior coronary disease, aspirin use, severe angina, ST changes, and troponin to estimate 14-day risk of death, myocardial infarction, or urgent revascularisation.

title: GRACE Risk Score
content: The GRACE score estimates in-hospital and 6-month mortality risk in acute coronary syndrome using variables including age, heart rate, blood pressure, creatinine, cardiac arrest at presentation, ST changes, elevated cardiac markers, and Killip class.

title: Aspirin in Suspected Acute Coronary Syndrome
content: A loading dose of aspirin, typically 300 mg chewed or dispersed, should be given as soon as possible to all patients with suspected acute coronary syndrome unless contraindicated, as it reduces mortality through antiplatelet effect.

title: Dual Antiplatelet Therapy in ACS
content: In addition to aspirin, a second antiplatelet agent such as ticagrelor, prasugrel, or clopidogrel is typically given in acute coronary syndrome, with the specific agent and timing influenced by planned management strategy and bleeding risk.

title: Anticoagulation in Acute Coronary Syndrome
content: Parenteral anticoagulation, such as unfractionated heparin or low molecular weight heparin, is used alongside antiplatelet therapy in acute coronary syndrome to reduce thrombus propagation, with the choice of agent influenced by planned revascularisation strategy.

title: Oxygen Therapy in Acute Coronary Syndrome
content: Routine supplemental oxygen is not recommended in ACS patients with SpO2 of 94% or above, as hyperoxia may worsen myocardial injury. Oxygen should be titrated to a target saturation rather than given indiscriminately.

title: Nitrates in Chest Pain Management
content: Sublingual or intravenous nitrates can relieve ischaemic chest pain by reducing preload and coronary vasospasm, but are contraindicated in suspected right ventricular infarction, severe hypotension, and recent use of phosphodiesterase inhibitors.

title: Morphine Use in Acute Coronary Syndrome
content: Morphine may be used for analgesia in ACS but has been associated with delayed absorption of oral antiplatelet agents and should be used cautiously, reserved for patients with ongoing significant pain despite nitrates.

title: Right-Sided ECG Leads for Inferior MI
content: In patients with inferior ST-elevation myocardial infarction, right-sided ECG leads should be obtained to assess for right ventricular involvement, which has important implications for management, including avoidance of nitrates and preload-reducing agents.

title: Posterior Myocardial Infarction
content: Posterior myocardial infarction can be missed on a standard 12-lead ECG, presenting with ST depression in anterior leads. Posterior leads (V7 to V9) should be considered when posterior MI is suspected clinically, as it represents a STEMI equivalent.

title: Wellens Syndrome
content: Wellens syndrome describes a specific T-wave pattern in the precordial leads indicating critical proximal left anterior descending artery stenosis. Patients may be pain-free at the time of ECG but are at high risk of impending anterior MI.

title: De Winter ECG Pattern
content: The de Winter pattern is an ECG variant indicating acute occlusion of the left anterior descending artery, characterised by upsloping ST depression and tall symmetric T waves in precordial leads, and should be treated as a STEMI equivalent requiring urgent reperfusion.

title: Aortic Dissection — Clinical Recognition
content: Aortic dissection classically presents with sudden severe tearing chest or back pain, a pulse deficit between limbs, and a blood pressure differential between arms. CT angiography is the investigation of choice when dissection is suspected.

title: Aortic Dissection — Imaging Findings
content: A widened mediastinum on chest X-ray can suggest aortic dissection but has limited sensitivity; CT angiography or transoesophageal echocardiography is required to confirm the diagnosis and define the extent of dissection.

title: Aortic Dissection Classification and Management
content: Type A dissections, involving the ascending aorta, require emergency surgical repair, while uncomplicated Type B dissections, confined to the descending aorta, are often managed medically with blood pressure and heart rate control.

title: Pulmonary Embolism as a Chest Pain Differential
content: Pulmonary embolism should be considered in patients with chest pain, particularly pleuritic pain, accompanied by breathlessness, tachycardia, or risk factors such as recent immobility, surgery, or malignancy. The Wells score helps guide further investigation.

title: Wells Score and PERC Rule for Pulmonary Embolism
content: The Wells score stratifies pre-test probability of pulmonary embolism, while the PERC rule helps identify very low-risk patients in whom further testing for PE may be safely avoided without measuring a D-dimer.

title: D-Dimer and CT Pulmonary Angiography
content: In patients with low to moderate pre-test probability of pulmonary embolism, a negative D-dimer can safely exclude the diagnosis. Patients with high pre-test probability or a positive D-dimer require CT pulmonary angiography for definitive diagnosis.

title: Pericarditis — Recognition
content: Acute pericarditis presents with sharp chest pain that worsens lying flat and improves sitting forward, often with a pericardial friction rub on auscultation. ECG typically shows widespread saddle-shaped ST elevation and PR depression.

title: Myocarditis as a Chest Pain Differential
content: Myocarditis can present with chest pain, breathlessness, and arrhythmia, often following a viral illness. Troponin may be elevated and echocardiography may show reduced ventricular function, distinguishing it from typical ischaemic chest pain.

title: Oesophageal Rupture (Boerhaave Syndrome)
content: Oesophageal rupture classically follows forceful vomiting and presents with severe chest pain, subcutaneous emphysema, and signs of mediastinitis. It is a surgical emergency with high mortality if diagnosis or treatment is delayed.

title: Pneumothorax as a Chest Pain Differential
content: Spontaneous pneumothorax presents with sudden onset pleuritic chest pain and breathlessness, often in tall thin young patients or those with underlying lung disease. Tension pneumothorax requires immediate needle decompression rather than imaging confirmation.

title: Musculoskeletal Chest Pain
content: Musculoskeletal chest pain is typically reproducible on palpation, related to movement or specific positions, and not associated with exertion or systemic symptoms. It is a diagnosis of exclusion after time-critical causes have been considered.

title: Gastro-Oesophageal Reflux as a Chest Pain Mimic
content: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease can cause burning retrosternal chest pain that may mimic cardiac ischaemia. A trial of antacid therapy and careful exclusion of cardiac causes is required before attributing chest pain to reflux.

title: Cocaine-Induced Chest Pain
content: Cocaine use can cause coronary vasospasm and myocardial infarction even in young patients without traditional risk factors. Beta-blockers are generally avoided due to risk of unopposed alpha-adrenergic vasoconstriction; benzodiazepines and nitrates are preferred.

title: Atypical Chest Pain Presentation in Women
content: Women with acute coronary syndrome more frequently present with atypical symptoms such as fatigue, nausea, or back pain rather than classic crushing chest pain, which can contribute to delayed recognition and treatment.

title: Silent Myocardial Ischaemia in Diabetic Patients
content: Patients with diabetes may experience silent or atypical myocardial ischaemia due to autonomic neuropathy, presenting with breathlessness, fatigue, or vague malaise rather than classic chest pain. A high index of suspicion is required.

title: Chest Pain in Elderly Patients
content: Elderly patients with acute coronary syndrome may present with breathlessness, confusion, or syncope rather than classic chest pain. Atypical presentation should not lower clinical suspicion, given the higher baseline cardiovascular risk in this group.

title: Serial Troponin Sampling
content: Serial troponin measurements, typically at presentation and again at 1 to 3 hours depending on the assay, are used to detect the characteristic rise and fall pattern of myocardial infarction and to distinguish it from chronically elevated troponin.

title: Chest Pain Observation Unit Pathways
content: Low to intermediate risk chest pain patients may be managed in a dedicated observation or chest pain unit pathway involving serial troponin testing, ECG monitoring, and risk stratification, allowing safe discharge without prolonged inpatient admission.

title: Cardiovascular Risk Factor Assessment in Chest Pain
content: Assessment of cardiovascular risk factors, including smoking, hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidaemia, family history of premature coronary disease, and prior cardiac events, informs both immediate risk stratification and discharge planning for chest pain patients.

title: Discharge Criteria After Chest Pain Workup
content: Patients may be considered for discharge after chest pain workup if serial troponins are negative, ECG shows no dynamic ischaemic change, risk scores indicate low risk, and symptoms have resolved, with clear safety-netting advice provided.

title: Outpatient Functional Testing After Chest Pain Discharge
content: Patients discharged after a low-risk chest pain workup may be referred for outpatient functional testing, such as exercise stress testing or CT coronary angiography, to further evaluate for underlying coronary artery disease.

title: CT Coronary Angiography in Chest Pain Assessment
content: CT coronary angiography provides high sensitivity for excluding significant coronary artery disease in patients with low to intermediate risk chest pain and stable presentation, supporting more rapid and confident discharge decisions.

title: Killip Classification in Acute Coronary Syndrome
content: The Killip classification stratifies patients with acute myocardial infarction by clinical signs of heart failure, ranging from Class I with no signs of failure to Class IV with cardiogenic shock, and predicts in-hospital mortality.

title: Cardiogenic Shock Following Myocardial Infarction
content: Cardiogenic shock following myocardial infarction presents with hypotension, poor peripheral perfusion, and pulmonary oedema due to severe left ventricular dysfunction. It carries high mortality and may require mechanical circulatory support alongside revascularisation.

title: Recreational Drug and Stimulant History in Chest Pain
content: A history of recreational stimulant use, including cocaine and amphetamines, should be specifically sought in younger patients presenting with chest pain, as these substances can precipitate myocardial infarction independent of traditional atherosclerotic risk factors.

title: Chest Pain with Syncope
content: Chest pain accompanied by syncope is a high-risk combination suggesting possible significant arrhythmia, aortic stenosis, pulmonary embolism, or aortic dissection, and warrants urgent and thorough evaluation rather than reassurance.

title: New Onset Arrhythmia with Chest Pain
content: New onset atrial fibrillation or other significant arrhythmia accompanying chest pain may indicate underlying ischaemia or structural heart disease, and requires ECG monitoring, electrolyte assessment, and consideration of cardiology involvement.

title: Acute Respiratory Distress — Initial Assessment
content: Patients with acute respiratory distress require immediate assessment of airway patency, breathing effort, and oxygenation. A respiratory rate above 25 per minute with SpO2 below 92% indicates severe distress requiring urgent intervention.

title: Oxygen Therapy Targets
content: For most acutely unwell patients, oxygen should be titrated to maintain SpO2 between 94 and 98%. For patients with COPD or known hypercapnic respiratory failure, the target range is 88 to 92% to avoid suppressing hypoxic respiratory drive.

title: Acute Severe Asthma — Recognition
content: Acute severe asthma is characterised by peak expiratory flow of 33 to 50% of best or predicted, respiratory rate above 25, heart rate above 110 bpm, and inability to complete sentences in one breath, requiring immediate bronchodilator therapy.

title: Life-Threatening Asthma Features
content: Life-threatening asthma features include peak flow below 33% of best or predicted, a silent chest, cyanosis, poor respiratory effort, exhaustion, confusion, and SpO2 below 92%, indicating need for immediate aggressive treatment and likely intensive care involvement.

title: Silent Chest as an Ominous Sign
content: A silent chest on auscultation in a patient with acute asthma does not indicate improvement but rather such severe airflow obstruction that air movement is insufficient to generate audible wheeze, representing a pre-arrest state requiring immediate escalation.

title: Near-Fatal Asthma and Status Asthmaticus
content: Near-fatal asthma is defined by a raised PaCO2 or the need for mechanical ventilation, while status asthmaticus refers to a severe asthma attack that fails to respond to standard bronchodilator therapy. Both require intensive care management.

title: COPD Exacerbation Management
content: Acute exacerbation of COPD is managed with controlled oxygen targeting SpO2 88 to 92%, inhaled short-acting bronchodilators, systemic corticosteroids, and antibiotics if bacterial infection is suspected. Non-invasive ventilation is indicated for persistent hypercapnic respiratory failure.

title: Non-Invasive Ventilation Indications
content: NIV using CPAP or BiPAP is indicated in acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema and hypercapnic respiratory failure in COPD with pH 7.25 to 7.35. Contraindications include reduced consciousness, vomiting, facial injury, and inability to protect the airway.

title: CPAP Versus BiPAP
content: CPAP delivers a continuous level of positive airway pressure throughout the respiratory cycle and is most useful for hypoxaemic conditions such as pulmonary oedema, while BiPAP provides different pressures for inspiration and expiration and is preferred for hypercapnic respiratory failure.

title: Tension Pneumothorax — Emergency Recognition
content: Tension pneumothorax presents with severe respiratory distress, tracheal deviation away from the affected side, absent breath sounds, hypotension, and raised jugular venous pressure. Immediate needle decompression is life-saving and must not wait for imaging.

title: Simple Pneumothorax Management
content: A small primary spontaneous pneumothorax without significant breathlessness may be managed conservatively with observation, while larger pneumothoraces or those causing significant symptoms typically require needle aspiration or chest drain insertion.

title: Acute Cardiogenic Pulmonary Oedema Management
content: Acute pulmonary oedema is treated with the patient sitting upright, high-flow oxygen, CPAP where available, intravenous diuretics, and nitrates if systolic blood pressure remains adequate, with underlying causes such as ACS or arrhythmia identified and treated.

title: High-Flow Nasal Oxygen
content: High-flow nasal oxygen delivers heated humidified oxygen at flows up to 60 L/min with high FiO2, reducing work of breathing and providing mild positive pressure, and may reduce intubation rates in selected patients with hypoxaemic respiratory failure.

title: Anaphylaxis with Respiratory Compromise
content: Anaphylaxis causing bronchospasm or airway oedema is life-threatening. Immediate intramuscular adrenaline is first-line treatment, with inhaled bronchodilators, antihistamines, and corticosteroids as adjuncts, and observation for at least 6 to 12 hours due to biphasic reaction risk.

title: Type 1 Versus Type 2 Respiratory Failure
content: Type 1 respiratory failure is hypoxaemia with normal or low carbon dioxide, caused by ventilation-perfusion mismatch such as pneumonia or pulmonary embolism. Type 2 involves hypoxaemia with hypercapnia, resulting from alveolar hypoventilation as seen in COPD or neuromuscular disease.

title: Pneumonia Severity Assessment — CURB-65
content: The CURB-65 score assesses pneumonia severity using Confusion, Urea above 7 mmol/L, Respiratory rate of 30 or above, Blood pressure below 90/60 mmHg, and age 65 or above, guiding decisions about hospital admission and treatment intensity.

title: Upper Airway Obstruction — Croup
content: Croup presents with a barking cough, inspiratory stridor, and hoarse voice in young children, usually due to viral laryngotracheobronchitis. Mild cases respond to oral dexamethasone, while severe cases with significant stridor at rest require nebulised adrenaline.

title: Epiglottitis — Recognition and Management
content: Acute epiglottitis presents with high fever, drooling, stridor, and a preference to sit upright in the 'tripod' position. It is a life-threatening airway emergency requiring immediate senior anaesthetic and ENT involvement, and the throat should not be examined directly due to risk of complete obstruction.

title: Bronchiolitis in Infants
content: Bronchiolitis presents with coryzal symptoms progressing to cough, wheeze, and respiratory distress in infants under 12 months, most commonly caused by respiratory syncytial virus. Management is largely supportive, with oxygen and feeding support for more severely affected infants.

title: Foreign Body Airway Obstruction in Children
content: Sudden onset coughing, choking, or stridor in a previously well child should raise suspicion for foreign body aspiration. Complete airway obstruction with inability to cough or speak requires immediate back blows and abdominal or chest thrusts depending on age.

title: Choking Management in Adults
content: For conscious adults with severe choking and ineffective cough, five back blows followed by five abdominal thrusts are recommended, repeated until the obstruction clears or the patient becomes unconscious, at which point CPR should be commenced.

title: Stridor as a Sign of Upper Airway Obstruction
content: Stridor, a high-pitched inspiratory sound, indicates significant upper airway narrowing and should prompt urgent assessment for causes such as foreign body, anaphylaxis, epiglottitis, or airway burns, with early anaesthetic involvement for definitive airway management.

title: ARDS — Berlin Definition
content: Acute respiratory distress syndrome is defined by acute onset within one week, bilateral opacities on chest imaging not fully explained by cardiac failure, and hypoxaemia categorised as mild, moderate, or severe based on the PaO2/FiO2 ratio.

title: Lung-Protective Ventilation in ARDS
content: Mechanically ventilated patients with ARDS benefit from lung-protective ventilation strategies using low tidal volumes of approximately 6 mL/kg of predicted body weight to reduce ventilator-induced lung injury.

title: Prone Positioning in Severe ARDS
content: Prone positioning improves oxygenation and has been shown to reduce mortality in patients with severe ARDS and a PaO2/FiO2 ratio below 150, by improving ventilation-perfusion matching in the dependent lung regions.

title: Smoke Inhalation Injury
content: Smoke inhalation can cause thermal airway injury, carbon monoxide poisoning, and cyanide toxicity. Signs such as facial burns, soot in the airway, or singed nasal hair should prompt early consideration of airway protection before swelling progresses.

title: Carbon Monoxide Poisoning and Pulse Oximetry
content: Standard pulse oximetry cannot distinguish carboxyhaemoglobin from oxyhaemoglobin and may show a falsely normal or near-normal reading in carbon monoxide poisoning. Co-oximetry or blood gas carboxyhaemoglobin measurement is required for accurate assessment.

title: Near-Drowning and Respiratory Compromise
content: Near-drowning can cause significant pulmonary injury and hypoxaemia even in patients who appear initially well, and observation for a period of hours is recommended to monitor for delayed respiratory deterioration.

title: Flail Chest and Chest Wall Injury
content: Flail chest occurs when multiple adjacent ribs are fractured in two or more places, causing paradoxical chest wall movement and significantly impaired ventilation. Management focuses on analgesia, oxygenation, and consideration of ventilatory support for severe cases.

title: Haemothorax
content: Haemothorax presents with respiratory distress and signs of hypovolaemia following chest trauma, with reduced breath sounds and dullness to percussion on the affected side. Chest drain insertion is required, with surgical exploration considered for large or ongoing blood loss.

title: Diaphragmatic Rupture
content: Diaphragmatic rupture following blunt or penetrating trauma can cause abdominal contents to herniate into the thoracic cavity, presenting with respiratory distress and bowel sounds audible in the chest. CT imaging or surgical exploration confirms the diagnosis.

title: Obesity Hypoventilation Syndrome
content: Obesity hypoventilation syndrome causes chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure due to reduced chest wall compliance and impaired respiratory drive in severely obese patients, and may require long-term non-invasive ventilatory support.

title: Neuromuscular Respiratory Failure
content: Conditions such as Guillain-Barré syndrome and myasthenic crisis can cause progressive respiratory muscle weakness leading to respiratory failure. Serial measurement of vital capacity and negative inspiratory force helps anticipate the need for ventilatory support before overt hypoxaemia develops.

title: Pleural Effusion and Breathlessness
content: Large pleural effusions can cause significant breathlessness through lung compression. Diagnostic and therapeutic thoracentesis can rapidly relieve symptoms, with the underlying cause, such as malignancy, infection, or heart failure, guiding further management.

title: Acute Exacerbation of Pulmonary Fibrosis
content: Acute exacerbation of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis presents with rapidly worsening breathlessness and new diffuse opacities on imaging in a patient with known fibrotic lung disease, and carries a high mortality rate despite supportive treatment.

title: Accessory Muscle Use as a Sign of Respiratory Distress
content: Use of accessory muscles, including the sternocleidomastoid and intercostal muscles, indicates increased work of breathing and should prompt escalation of respiratory support and closer monitoring.

title: Tripod Positioning
content: Patients in significant respiratory distress often adopt a tripod position, leaning forward with hands on the knees, to optimise accessory muscle use and maximise chest expansion. This posture should be recognised as a sign of significant distress.

title: Paradoxical Breathing Pattern
content: A paradoxical breathing pattern, where the abdomen moves inward during inspiration rather than outward, suggests diaphragmatic fatigue or paralysis and is an ominous sign of impending respiratory failure requiring urgent ventilatory support.

title: Cyanosis as a Clinical Sign
content: Central cyanosis, visible in the lips and tongue, typically indicates significant hypoxaemia, often with oxygen saturation below 85%, and represents a late and unreliable sign that should not be relied upon to exclude earlier hypoxaemia.

title: Arterial Blood Gas Interpretation Basics
content: Arterial blood gas analysis assesses pH, PaO2, PaCO2, and bicarbonate to characterise the type and severity of respiratory failure and to identify accompanying metabolic disturbance, guiding decisions about oxygen and ventilatory support.

title: Capnography Monitoring
content: End-tidal carbon dioxide monitoring via capnography provides continuous, real-time assessment of ventilation and is particularly valuable during procedural sedation, airway management, and transport of critically unwell respiratory patients.

title: Bag-Valve-Mask Ventilation Technique
content: Effective bag-valve-mask ventilation requires a good mask seal, an open airway often achieved with a head tilt-chin lift or jaw thrust, and delivery of breaths at an appropriate rate and volume to avoid gastric insufflation and barotrauma.

title: Indications for Endotracheal Intubation
