content: Indications for intubation include inability to protect the airway, refractory hypoxaemia or hypercapnia despite non-invasive measures, exhaustion from increased work of breathing, and a decreasing level of consciousness threatening airway patency.

title: Rapid Sequence Induction Considerations
content: Rapid sequence induction for emergency intubation involves pre-oxygenation, simultaneous administration of an induction agent and a rapid-onset paralytic, and application of cricoid pressure in some protocols to minimise aspiration risk during airway control.

title: Oxygen Delivery Devices — Nasal Cannula to Non-Rebreathe Mask
content: Oxygen delivery devices range from low-flow nasal cannula providing modest FiO2 increases, through simple face masks and Venturi masks for controlled concentrations, to non-rebreathe masks capable of delivering close to 100% oxygen for the most hypoxaemic patients.

title: Venturi Masks for Controlled Oxygen Delivery
content: Venturi masks deliver a fixed, predictable concentration of oxygen regardless of the patient's respiratory pattern, making them particularly useful for patients with COPD who require precise oxygen titration to avoid hypercapnia.

title: Weaning Oxygen Therapy
content: Once a patient's respiratory condition improves, oxygen therapy should be weaned in a stepwise fashion with monitoring of saturation, to the lowest concentration that maintains target SpO2, avoiding both hypoxaemia and unnecessary hyperoxia.

title: Discharge Criteria After Asthma Exacerbation
content: Patients may be considered for discharge after an asthma exacerbation once peak flow has returned to at least 70% of best or predicted, symptoms have substantially improved, and they have been stable on their usual or stepped-down inhaler regimen for several hours.

title: Discharge Criteria After COPD Exacerbation
content: Discharge after a COPD exacerbation requires clinical stability on usual home oxygen requirements, ability to manage activities of daily living, and a clear follow-up and self-management plan, including inhaler technique review.

title: Smoking Cessation Counselling in Respiratory Disease
content: Brief smoking cessation advice should be offered to all patients presenting with respiratory illness who smoke, as cessation is the single most effective intervention to slow progression of COPD and reduce future exacerbations.

title: Vaccination for COPD Exacerbation Prevention
content: Annual influenza vaccination and pneumococcal vaccination are recommended for patients with COPD to reduce the frequency and severity of future exacerbations triggered by respiratory infection.

title: Pulmonary Rehabilitation Referral
content: Patients recovering from a COPD exacerbation should be referred for pulmonary rehabilitation, a structured exercise and education programme shown to improve breathlessness, exercise capacity, and quality of life.

title: Work of Breathing Assessment in Children
content: Assessment of work of breathing in children includes observing for nasal flaring, grunting, recession (intercostal or subcostal), and head bobbing in infants, which are sensitive indicators of significant respiratory distress.

title: FAST Assessment for Stroke
content: The FAST acronym is the primary public-facing tool for stroke recognition: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call emergency services. Any sudden onset neurological deficit should be treated as a stroke until proven otherwise.

title: BE-FAST Assessment
content: BE-FAST expands FAST with Balance disturbance and Eyes (sudden visual changes) to improve sensitivity for posterior circulation strokes, which classic FAST often misses, given that vertigo and visual symptoms are not captured by the original tool.

title: ROSIER Scale for Stroke Recognition
content: The ROSIER scale helps differentiate stroke from stroke mimics in the emergency department, awarding points for asymmetric facial, arm, or leg weakness, and speech disturbance, while subtracting points for seizure activity or loss of consciousness. A score above 0 suggests stroke.

title: NIHSS — Stroke Severity Assessment
content: The National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale quantifies stroke severity across 11 items including consciousness, gaze, visual fields, facial palsy, motor function, ataxia, sensory loss, language, and neglect, with scores from 0 to 42. A score of 6 or above generally indicates moderate-to-severe stroke.

title: Thrombolysis Eligibility in Ischaemic Stroke
content: IV alteplase is indicated for ischaemic stroke with disabling neurological deficit treatable within 4.5 hours of known symptom onset. Absolute contraindications include haemorrhagic stroke, recent major surgery, active bleeding, and uncontrolled hypertension above 185/110 mmHg.

title: Mechanical Thrombectomy Criteria
content: Mechanical thrombectomy is indicated for large vessel occlusion ischaemic stroke treatable within 6 hours of onset, extending up to 24 hours in selected patients using perfusion imaging. Functional independence before stroke and absence of large established infarct core are key selection criteria.

title: Blood Pressure Management Before Thrombolysis
content: Blood pressure must be brought below 185/110 mmHg before alteplase administration in eligible ischaemic stroke patients, as uncontrolled hypertension increases the risk of haemorrhagic transformation after thrombolysis.

title: Blood Pressure Management Without Thrombolysis
content: For ischaemic stroke patients not receiving thrombolysis, blood pressure should only be actively lowered if above 220/120 mmHg or in the presence of a hypertensive emergency, since aggressive reduction can worsen penumbral ischaemia and outcomes.

title: Haemorrhagic Stroke — Recognition and Management
content: Intracerebral haemorrhage presents with sudden headache, focal deficit, reduced consciousness, and vomiting. CT head differentiates it from ischaemic stroke. Thrombolytics and anticoagulants are absolutely contraindicated once haemorrhage is confirmed.

title: Blood Pressure Targets in Intracerebral Haemorrhage
content: For intracerebral haemorrhage with presenting systolic blood pressure of 150 to 220 mmHg, lowering to below 140 mmHg within one hour is recommended, balancing the goals of limiting haematoma expansion against the risk of cerebral hypoperfusion.

title: Anticoagulant Reversal in Haemorrhagic Stroke
content: Patients on warfarin presenting with intracerebral haemorrhage require urgent reversal with prothrombin complex concentrate and vitamin K, while specific reversal agents exist for direct oral anticoagulants. Rapid reversal limits haematoma expansion and improves outcomes.

title: TIA — Urgent Assessment and ABCD2 Score
content: Transient ischaemic attack carries a significant risk of early stroke, approximately 10% within 48 hours. The ABCD2 score estimates short-term stroke risk based on Age, Blood pressure, Clinical features, Duration, and Diabetes, with high scores warranting urgent specialist review.

title: TIA with Atrial Fibrillation
content: Patients with TIA found to have atrial fibrillation should be assessed by a stroke specialist within 24 hours regardless of ABCD2 score, given the elevated stroke risk associated with cardioembolic sources.

title: Stroke Mimics
content: Stroke mimics account for up to 30% of stroke presentations and include hypoglycaemia, Todd's paresis after seizure, complex migraine, hypertensive encephalopathy, and functional neurological disorder. Blood glucose must be checked in all suspected stroke patients before imaging.

title: Hypoglycaemia as a Stroke Mimic
content: Hypoglycaemia can cause focal neurological deficits indistinguishable from stroke, including hemiparesis and speech disturbance. Correcting hypoglycaemia immediately may rapidly resolve symptoms and avoid unnecessary thrombolysis.

title: Posterior Circulation Stroke
content: Posterior circulation strokes account for approximately 20% of all strokes and are frequently missed because FAST criteria have poor sensitivity for these presentations. Key symptoms include sudden vertigo, diplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia, ataxia, and bilateral limb weakness.

title: Cerebellar Stroke Recognition
content: Cerebellar stroke presents with sudden ataxia, vertigo, vomiting, and headache, and can be life-threatening due to risk of brainstem compression or hydrocephalus from cerebellar swelling, sometimes requiring urgent neurosurgical decompression.

title: Last Known Well Time
content: The 'last known well' time, defined as the last time the patient was witnessed to be at their neurological baseline, is used as the reference point for determining eligibility for thrombolysis and thrombectomy when exact onset time is uncertain.

title: Wake-Up Stroke and Unknown Onset Stroke
content: For strokes discovered upon waking with unknown true onset time, advanced imaging such as MRI diffusion-weighted and FLAIR sequence mismatch can identify patients likely to be within a treatable time window for thrombolysis.

title: CT Versus MRI in Hyperacute Stroke
content: Non-contrast CT head is the first-line imaging modality in hyperacute stroke due to speed and wide availability, primarily to exclude haemorrhage, while MRI offers greater sensitivity for early ischaemic change and posterior circulation strokes when time and availability allow.

title: ASPECTS Score for Early Ischaemic Change
content: The Alberta Stroke Program Early CT Score (ASPECTS) quantifies early ischaemic change on non-contrast CT across ten brain regions, with lower scores indicating more extensive infarction and influencing eligibility for thrombectomy.

title: Clinical Prediction Scores for Large Vessel Occlusion
content: Pre-hospital scales such as the Cincinnati Prehospital Stroke Severity Scale and the Rapid Arterial Occlusion Evaluation scale help paramedics predict large vessel occlusion, supporting decisions to bypass to a comprehensive stroke centre capable of thrombectomy.

title: Cervical Artery Dissection
content: Carotid or vertebral artery dissection can cause stroke, particularly in younger patients, and may present with neck pain, headache, Horner's syndrome, or pulsatile tinnitus preceding focal neurological deficit.

title: Cerebral Venous Sinus Thrombosis
content: Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis presents with headache, seizures, and focal deficits, often in the context of risk factors such as pregnancy, oral contraceptive use, or hypercoagulable states. CT or MR venography is required for diagnosis, as standard arterial imaging may appear normal.

title: Subarachnoid Haemorrhage — Clinical Presentation
content: Subarachnoid haemorrhage classically presents with sudden severe 'thunderclap' headache, often described as the worst headache of the patient's life, sometimes accompanied by neck stiffness, photophobia, or reduced consciousness.

title: Subarachnoid Haemorrhage — Investigation
content: Non-contrast CT head performed within 6 hours of headache onset has very high sensitivity for subarachnoid haemorrhage. Lumbar puncture for xanthochromia should be considered if CT is negative but clinical suspicion remains high beyond this window.

title: Hunt-Hess Grading for Subarachnoid Haemorrhage
content: The Hunt-Hess scale grades the clinical severity of subarachnoid haemorrhage from grade 1, representing minimal symptoms, to grade 5, representing deep coma, guiding prognosis and management decisions including timing of intervention.

title: Stroke Unit Care
content: Admission to a dedicated stroke unit, providing coordinated multidisciplinary care, has been shown to reduce mortality and disability after stroke compared with care on a general medical ward, independent of thrombolysis or thrombectomy use.

title: Door-to-Needle Time Targets
content: Stroke centres aim for a door-to-needle time of 60 minutes or less from arrival to thrombolysis administration, with leading centres achieving times under 30 minutes through streamlined hyperacute stroke pathways.

title: Code Stroke Activation Criteria
content: A 'code stroke' or hyperacute stroke alert is activated for patients presenting with sudden focal neurological deficit within the treatment window, mobilising rapid CT imaging, stroke team assessment, and preparation for thrombolysis or thrombectomy.

title: Telestroke Services
content: Telestroke networks allow remote stroke specialists to assess patients via video link and guide thrombolysis decisions in hospitals without on-site stroke expertise, extending access to time-critical treatment in rural or under-resourced areas.

title: Post-Thrombolysis Monitoring
content: After thrombolysis administration, patients require frequent neurological observation and blood pressure monitoring, typically every 15 minutes for the first 2 hours, to detect early signs of haemorrhagic transformation or other complications.

title: Haemorrhagic Transformation After Thrombolysis
content: Sudden neurological deterioration, new headache, or rising blood pressure after thrombolysis should raise immediate suspicion for haemorrhagic transformation, requiring urgent repeat CT imaging and discontinuation of any further thrombolytic infusion.

title: Malignant MCA Syndrome and Decompressive Craniectomy
content: Malignant middle cerebral artery syndrome involves massive infarction causing life-threatening cerebral oedema and raised intracranial pressure. Decompressive craniectomy can be life-saving in selected younger patients when performed within a defined early time window.

title: Locked-In Syndrome
content: Locked-in syndrome results from a brainstem stroke causing quadriplegia and inability to speak while consciousness and cognition remain intact, typically with preserved vertical eye movement and blinking as the only means of communication.

title: Seizures at Stroke Onset
content: Seizures occurring at the onset of a focal neurological deficit can complicate stroke diagnosis, as post-ictal (Todd's) paresis can mimic stroke, while seizures can also be the presenting feature of an acute stroke itself, particularly haemorrhagic stroke.

title: Swallow Assessment After Stroke
content: All stroke patients should undergo a formal swallow screen before oral intake, as dysphagia is common after stroke and significantly increases the risk of aspiration pneumonia if unrecognised.

title: Aspiration Pneumonia Prevention After Stroke
content: Nil-by-mouth status and speech and language therapy assessment for patients failing initial swallow screening reduce the risk of aspiration pneumonia, a leading cause of post-stroke morbidity and mortality.

title: Venous Thromboembolism Prophylaxis After Stroke
content: Mechanical prophylaxis such as intermittent pneumatic compression is recommended for immobile stroke patients to reduce venous thromboembolism risk, with pharmacological prophylaxis used cautiously given bleeding risk, particularly after haemorrhagic stroke.

title: Secondary Stroke Prevention — Antiplatelet and Statin Therapy
content: Following ischaemic stroke or TIA, antiplatelet therapy and high-intensity statin treatment are initiated for secondary prevention, reducing the risk of recurrent stroke and other cardiovascular events.

title: Anticoagulation for Cardioembolic Stroke
content: Patients with ischaemic stroke caused by atrial fibrillation are typically transitioned from antiplatelet to anticoagulant therapy for secondary prevention, with timing influenced by infarct size and haemorrhagic transformation risk.

title: Carotid Endarterectomy Indications
content: Carotid endarterectomy is indicated for symptomatic carotid stenosis of 50% or greater, ideally performed within two weeks of the qualifying event, as the benefit of surgery diminishes with delay after the index stroke or TIA.

title: Stroke Rehabilitation
content: Early, intensive, multidisciplinary rehabilitation involving physiotherapy, occupational therapy, and speech and language therapy improves functional recovery after stroke and should begin as soon as the patient is medically stable.

title: Stroke in Pregnancy
content: Pregnancy and the postpartum period carry an increased risk of both ischaemic and haemorrhagic stroke, related to hypercoagulability and conditions such as pre-eclampsia. Imaging and treatment decisions require careful balancing of maternal and fetal considerations.

title: Stroke in Children
content: Paediatric stroke is rare but often under-recognised due to non-specific presentation and lower index of clinical suspicion. Underlying causes differ from adults and include congenital heart disease, sickle cell disease, and arteriopathies.

title: Stroke Risk Factors
content: Major modifiable risk factors for stroke include hypertension, atrial fibrillation, diabetes, smoking, hyperlipidaemia, and carotid artery disease. Identification and management of these factors are central to both primary and secondary prevention.

title: Patent Foramen Ovale and Cryptogenic Stroke
content: In younger patients with stroke of undetermined cause, patent foramen ovale should be considered as a potential source of paradoxical embolism, particularly when no other risk factors are identified.

title: Stroke Code Imaging Pathway
content: A streamlined hyperacute stroke imaging pathway typically bypasses the standard emergency department queue, transferring the patient directly to CT on arrival to minimise delay to thrombolysis or thrombectomy decision-making.

title: ED Triage — Primary Survey
content: All patients arriving in the emergency department must undergo triage assessment within 15 minutes of arrival, immediately for those who appear critically ill. The primary survey follows an ABCDE approach: Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Disability, and Exposure.

title: Manchester Triage System
content: The Manchester Triage System is a standardised five-level triage tool assigning patients to Immediate (red, 0 minutes), Very Urgent (orange, 10 minutes), Urgent (yellow, 60 minutes), Standard (green, 120 minutes), or Non-Urgent (blue, 240 minutes) based on presenting complaint and discriminators.

title: Canadian Triage and Acuity Scale (CTAS)
content: CTAS is a five-level triage scale used widely in Canada, assigning resuscitation, emergent, urgent, less urgent, and non-urgent categories with corresponding maximum wait times of 0, 15, 30, 60, and 120 minutes respectively.

title: Australasian Triage Scale (ATS)
content: The Australasian Triage Scale assigns five categories with target times to medical assessment ranging from immediate for category 1 to 120 minutes for category 5, and is widely used across Australia and New Zealand to benchmark emergency department performance.

title: Comparing Five-Level Triage Systems
content: ESI, CTAS, ATS, and the Manchester Triage System all use five acuity levels but differ in their underlying logic: ESI emphasises resource prediction, while CTAS, ATS, and MTS rely more heavily on presenting complaint discriminators and target time-to-treatment.

title: Triage Documentation Standards
content: Triage documentation must include time of arrival, time of triage assessment, triage category assigned, presenting complaint, vital signs, and any immediate interventions initiated, supporting clinical safety, medicolegal accountability, and audit.

title: Undifferentiated Presentations at Triage
content: Patients presenting with vague complaints such as 'feeling unwell' require careful triage assessment, as these presentations may conceal serious pathology including sepsis, acute coronary syndrome, or pulmonary embolism.

title: Pain Assessment at Triage
content: Pain should be assessed using a validated tool such as the Numerical Rating Scale or Visual Analogue Scale, with reassessment after analgesia is given. Severe pain of 7 or above warrants prompt analgesia and may influence triage category.

title: Nurse-Initiated Analgesia Protocols
content: Many emergency departments empower triage nurses to initiate analgesia, such as paracetamol or weak opioids, under standing protocols before physician review, reducing time to pain relief for patients with significant pain at triage.

title: Paediatric Triage Adjustments
content: Children require age-specific vital sign reference ranges and behavioural assessment at triage. The Paediatric Assessment Triangle evaluates appearance, work of breathing, and circulation to the skin as a rapid global assessment.

title: Paediatric Red Flag Features at Triage
content: Infants with fever and a bulging fontanelle, non-blanching rash, or inconsolable crying require immediate escalation at triage, as these findings raise concern for serious bacterial infection or meningitis.

title: Broselow Tape for Paediatric Weight Estimation
content: The Broselow tape estimates a child's weight based on length, allowing rapid calculation of appropriate drug doses and equipment sizes in emergencies when an accurate weight is not immediately available.

title: Infection Control at Triage
content: Triage nurses must identify patients with potential infectious disease at first contact. Patients with fever and rash, respiratory symptoms, or diarrhoea and vomiting should be assessed for isolation need and provided with masks where appropriate.

title: Isolation Precautions for Specific Infectious Diseases
content: Patients with suspected airborne infections such as tuberculosis or measles require negative pressure isolation where available, while those with suspected norovirus or other contact-spread infections require standard contact precautions.

title: Escalation Pathways from Triage
content: Clear escalation pathways must exist from triage to appropriate care areas. ESI-1 patients go directly to the resuscitation room, while activation of major trauma, stroke, STEMI, or sepsis pathways can often be initiated directly by the triage nurse.

title: Waiting Room Safety Monitoring
content: Patients in the waiting room must be monitored regularly for deterioration. Repeat vital sign checks, nurse walk-throughs, and a clear process for patients or families to alert staff are essential safety measures.

title: Mental Health Presentations at Triage
content: Patients presenting with mental health crises must be triaged with the same urgency as medical emergencies. Active suicidal ideation with a plan or intent, severe agitation, or psychosis with risk to self or others requires immediate assessment.

title: Psychiatric Emergency Environment at Triage
content: A safe, low-stimulus environment should be provided for patients presenting with acute mental health crises, with security or psychiatric liaison staff available as needed to support both patient and staff safety.

title: Alcohol Withdrawal Assessment at Triage
content: Patients at risk of alcohol withdrawal should be screened using a validated tool such as the CIWA-Ar scale, as severe withdrawal can progress rapidly to seizures or delirium tremens, requiring close monitoring and timely treatment.

title: Intoxicated Patients at Triage
content: Intoxicated patients require careful assessment to distinguish simple intoxication from co-existing trauma, hypoglycaemia, or other medical emergencies, as altered behaviour may otherwise be wrongly attributed solely to alcohol or drug use.

title: Trauma Triage and Major Trauma Activation
content: Major trauma activation criteria typically include specific physiological parameters, anatomical injury patterns, and mechanism of injury factors, prompting immediate assembly of a trauma team and direct transfer to a resuscitation bay.

title: Mass Casualty Triage — START Method
content: Simple Triage and Rapid Treatment (START) is used in mass casualty incidents to rapidly sort patients into immediate, delayed, minor, or deceased categories based on ability to walk, breathing, circulation, and mental status, prioritising the greatest number of survivable casualties.

title: JumpSTART Paediatric Mass Casualty Triage
content: JumpSTART is a paediatric adaptation of the START mass casualty triage method, accounting for differing normal vital sign ranges and rescue breathing considerations specific to children in disaster settings.

title: Telephone Triage
content: Telephone triage services use structured algorithms to assess symptoms remotely and direct callers to emergency care, urgent care, primary care, or self-care, aiming to ensure patients reach the appropriate level of care without unnecessary emergency department attendance.

title: Fast-Track and Minor Injuries Units
content: Fast-track or minor injuries units allow low-acuity patients, typically ESI-4 and ESI-5, to be streamed away from the main emergency department for rapid assessment and treatment, improving overall department flow.

title: Ambulance Pre-Notification
content: Pre-hospital crews transporting time-critical patients, such as suspected stroke or STEMI, provide advance notification to the receiving emergency department, allowing the appropriate team and resources to be mobilised before patient arrival.

title: Emergency Department Overcrowding and Access Block
content: Access block, where admitted patients remain in the emergency department due to lack of inpatient beds, is a major driver of overcrowding and has been associated with worse patient outcomes, including increased mortality for high-acuity patients.

title: Boarding of Admitted Patients
content: Boarding refers to admitted patients remaining in the emergency department awaiting an inpatient bed. Prolonged boarding times are associated with increased risk of adverse events and should be monitored as a key department performance metric.

title: Left Without Being Seen Rates
content: Patients triaged as lower acuity are more likely to leave without being seen during periods of crowding. Monitoring this metric by triage category helps emergency departments identify safety risks and target service improvements.

title: Repeat Attenders and Frequent Flyers
content: Patients who attend the emergency department frequently for the same or related complaints may benefit from individualised care plans developed with input from primary care and relevant specialists to address underlying needs more effectively.

title: Safeguarding Concerns at Triage
content: Triage staff should be alert to safeguarding concerns in children, older adults, and vulnerable patients, including signs of neglect or abuse, and should know the local pathway for escalation to safeguarding leads.

title: Domestic Violence Screening
content: Routine, sensitive enquiry about domestic violence should be considered when injury patterns or presentation raise concern, with privacy ensured during the conversation and a clear pathway for referral to support services when disclosed.

title: Sexual Assault Presentations
content: Patients presenting after sexual assault require sensitive triage, prompt access to a private area, and early involvement of specialist forensic and psychological support services, alongside consideration of evidence preservation needs.

title: Forensic Evidence Preservation
content: In presentations involving potential criminal proceedings, such as assault or sexual violence, care should be taken to preserve forensic evidence where possible without delaying necessary urgent medical treatment.

title: Elderly Frailty Screening
content: Frailty screening tools such as the Clinical Frailty Scale can be applied at triage to identify older patients at higher risk of poor outcomes, supporting earlier comprehensive geriatric assessment and tailored disposition planning.

title: Delirium Screening at Triage
content: Tools such as the 4AT can be used to rapidly screen for delirium in older patients at triage, as delirium is common, frequently under-recognised, and may indicate an underlying acute illness such as infection.

title: Interpreter Use at Triage
content: Professional interpreter services, rather than family members, should be used whenever a language barrier could compromise accurate triage assessment, to ensure patient safety and avoid miscommunication of critical symptoms.

title: Cultural Competence in Triage Assessment
content: Triage staff should be aware that expression of pain and symptoms can vary across cultural backgrounds, and should avoid making assumptions about severity based solely on outward presentation or demeanour.

title: Patient Identification at Triage
content: Accurate patient identification using at least two identifiers, such as name and date of birth, should be confirmed at triage and reflected on identification wristbands to prevent errors throughout the episode of care.

title: Allergy Documentation at Triage
content: Allergy status, including specific reactions experienced, should be documented and clearly flagged at triage to prevent inadvertent administration of an allergenic medication during subsequent care.

title: Medication Reconciliation at Triage
content: Obtaining an accurate current medication list at triage, including over-the-counter and herbal remedies, supports safe prescribing and helps identify medication-related causes of the presenting complaint.

title: Vital Sign Equipment Calibration
content: Regular calibration and maintenance of triage equipment, including blood pressure monitors, thermometers, and pulse oximeters, is essential to ensure accurate vital sign measurement and reliable triage decision-making.

title: Hand Hygiene and PPE at Triage
content: Hand hygiene before and after each patient contact, along with appropriate use of personal protective equipment based on suspected transmission risk, is a fundamental infection control measure beginning at the point of triage.

title: Triage in Pandemic Surge Conditions
content: During pandemic surge, additional screening questions for infectious symptoms and travel or exposure history are often incorporated into triage, alongside separated pathways for patients with suspected transmissible respiratory illness.

title: CBRN Decontamination Triage
content: Patients exposed to chemical, biological, radiological, or nuclear hazards require decontamination before entering the main emergency department, with triage occurring in a designated decontamination area to prevent secondary contamination of staff and facilities.

title: Triage Time-to-Assessment Targets
content: Most triage systems set a target time from arrival to initial triage assessment, commonly within 10 to 15 minutes, with immediate assessment for patients who appear critically unwell on arrival.

title: Colour-Coded Triage Systems
content: Many triage systems use colour coding, such as red for immediate, orange for very urgent, yellow for urgent, and green for standard, to provide a rapid visual cue of patient acuity for all staff in the department.

title: Bed Management and Flow Coordination
content: A dedicated flow coordinator or bed manager role helps match incoming patient acuity with available department resources in real time, supporting timely movement of patients from triage to an appropriate care area.

title: Early Senior Assessment Models
content: Some emergency departments implement early senior clinician assessment at or near triage, sometimes called 'front-of-house' or 'rapid assessment' models, to expedite initial diagnostic decision-making for selected patients.

title: See-and-Treat Models
content: See-and-treat models allow suitable low-acuity patients to be assessed and managed in a single encounter at or near triage, reducing overall length of stay for minor injuries and illnesses.

title: Triage Nurse Competency Standards
content: Triage nurses are typically required to complete structured competency-based training and ongoing assessment, reflecting the complexity and patient safety implications of accurate acuity assignment.

title: Triage Accuracy Auditing
content: Regular audit of triage accuracy, comparing assigned acuity level against actual resource use and outcomes, helps identify patterns of under-triage or over-triage and informs targeted staff training.

title: Walk-In Versus Ambulance Arrival Prioritisation
content: Triage acuity should be determined by clinical presentation rather than mode of arrival; a walk-in patient may be higher acuity than an ambulance arrival, and triage processes must avoid assumptions based on arrival method alone.

title: Point-of-Care Glucose Testing at Triage
content: Point-of-care blood glucose testing is recommended at triage for any patient with altered mental status, as hypoglycaemia is rapidly reversible and can mimic numerous serious neurological and psychiatric conditions.

title: Urine Dipstick Testing at Triage
content: Urine dipstick testing is a non-invasive point-of-care test commonly initiated at triage for patients with abdominal pain, urinary symptoms, or suspected pregnancy complications, supporting earlier diagnostic clarification.

title: Pregnancy Testing at Triage
content: A urine or serum pregnancy test should be obtained at triage for any woman of childbearing age presenting with abdominal pain or vaginal bleeding, as pregnancy status significantly changes the differential diagnosis and management priorities.

title: ECG Acquisition Protocol for Chest Pain at Triage
content: Many triage protocols mandate that a 12-lead ECG be obtained within 10 minutes for any patient reporting chest pain, irrespective of formal triage category, to ensure time-critical cardiac diagnoses are not delayed.

title: Splinting and Immobilisation Protocols at Triage
content: Triage nurses commonly apply splints or immobilisation devices to suspected fractures before formal physician assessment, reducing pain and preventing further injury while the patient awaits definitive evaluation.

title: Wound Care Protocols at Triage
content: Standing protocols often allow triage nurses to clean and dress simple wounds, apply pressure dressings for bleeding, and assess tetanus immunisation status, supporting earlier initial wound management.

title: Triage Refusal of Care and Capacity Assessment
content: When a patient declines recommended assessment or treatment, triage staff should consider the patient's capacity to make that decision and document the discussion, while ensuring the patient understands the risks of declining care.

title: Triage Communication Skills
content: Effective triage communication includes clear explanation of expected waiting times, what to do if symptoms worsen, and reassurance, which has been shown to improve patient satisfaction and reduce anxiety during the waiting period.

title: Major Trauma Network Referral Criteria
content: Patients meeting defined anatomical, physiological, or mechanism-based criteria for major trauma should be referred or transferred to a designated major trauma centre according to regional trauma network protocols, even if this requires bypassing a closer facility.

title: Inter-Facility Transfer Protocols
content: Patients requiring transfer to another facility for specialist care should be accompanied by appropriately trained staff and equipment matched to their clinical acuity, with clear handover documentation provided to the receiving team.

title: Ambulance Diversion Protocols
content: During periods of severe overcrowding, some regions implement ambulance diversion protocols redirecting incoming ambulances to alternative facilities, although these measures are used cautiously due to potential impact on patient outcomes.

title: Surge Triage Staffing
content: During periods of high patient volume, deploying a second triage nurse or a triage support team helps maintain timely initial assessment and prevents delays in identifying high-acuity patients among a larger queue.

title: Rapid Assessment and Treatment Zones
content: Some departments use a dedicated rapid assessment and treatment zone immediately adjacent to triage, allowing brief initial interventions such as analgesia or fluids to begin before the patient is formally allocated to a treatment bay.

title: Triage in Rural and Remote Settings
content: Triage protocols in rural and remote facilities must account for limited on-site resources and longer transfer times to definitive care, often incorporating lower thresholds for early transfer arrangement for higher-acuity patients.

title: Telehealth-Supported Triage
content: Telehealth links to remote specialists or senior clinicians can support triage decision-making in smaller facilities, particularly for time-critical presentations such as stroke or trauma where local expertise may be limited.

title: Public Health Campaigns Supporting Appropriate ED Use
content: Public health campaigns encouraging patients to 'choose well' between emergency, urgent, and primary care services aim to reduce inappropriate low-acuity emergency department attendance and support more efficient health system use.

title: Patient Experience and Communication at Triage
content: Patient experience surveys consistently identify communication about waiting times and the reason for triage category as key drivers of satisfaction, independent of the actual clinical outcome of the visit.

title: Triage Reassessment for Patients Awaiting a Bed
content: Patients who have been triaged but remain in a waiting area or corridor due to bed unavailability should be periodically reassessed, since prolonged waiting itself is a risk factor for unrecognised deterioration.

title: Triage Category and Legal or Forensic Documentation
content: Accurate, contemporaneous triage documentation can be critical evidence in subsequent legal or coronial proceedings, reinforcing the importance of objective, timely, and complete record-keeping at the point of first contact.

title: Triage of Patients with Disabilities
content: Patients with sensory, cognitive, or physical disabilities may require adapted communication approaches at triage, and staff should ask the patient or their support person about preferred communication methods rather than assuming limitations.

title: Triage of Unaccompanied Minors
content: Unaccompanied minors presenting to the emergency department require specific safeguarding consideration, including attempts to contact a parent or guardian and assessment of the circumstances leading to the unaccompanied presentation.

title: Triage for Suspected Non-Accidental Injury in Children
content: Injury patterns inconsistent with the reported mechanism, delayed presentation, or multiple injuries of different ages should raise suspicion for non-accidental injury in children, triggering a structured safeguarding pathway.

title: Triage Risk Communication to Waiting Patients
content: Providing waiting patients with clear safety-netting advice, such as specific symptoms that should prompt immediately alerting staff, helps mitigate risk during periods when patients must wait beyond their target triage time.

title: Triage and Initial Sepsis Screening Integration
content: Many emergency departments embed a sepsis screening question set directly within the triage process, ensuring that any patient with possible infection and reduced physiological reserve is flagged for rapid escalation regardless of overall triage category.

title: Triage and Initial Stroke Screening Integration
content: Embedding a brief stroke screening tool such as FAST within the triage assessment for any patient with neurological symptoms helps ensure rapid activation of the hyperacute stroke pathway without waiting for full physician assessment.

title: Triage Quality Indicators
content: Common triage quality indicators include time to triage, time to pain assessment, accuracy of triage category against final disposition, and rate of unplanned re-attendance, used together to monitor and improve triage performance.

title: Triage in Single-Provider or Low-Resource Settings
content: In settings with a single available clinician, triage protocols should clearly define which presentations justify interrupting ongoing care of another patient, balancing fairness with the need to respond rapidly to genuine emergencies.

title: Continuous Quality Improvement of Triage Protocols
content: Triage protocols should be periodically reviewed and updated based on audit findings, emerging evidence, and incident review, ensuring the system continues to reliably identify patients at risk of serious illness or deterioration.
